EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below average rainfall allowed severe drought conditions to continue across portions of Molokai.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 severe drought conditions existed for leeward Molokai as rainfall remained below normal (average) for the month. There were no hydrological, agricultural or fire hazard impacts reported for leeward Molokai.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below average rainfall allowed severe to extreme drought conditions to continue across portions of Maui.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D3 severe drought existed over Maui's central valley and portions of Upcountry Maui. Since 9/4/25, below normal rainfall continued a Stage 2 water shortage over West Maui. Since  9/12/25, Upcountry Maui remained at a Stage 1 water shortage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Eruption Episode 41 began at 11:10 a.m. on Saturday, January 24, 2026, in the Halemaumau crater caused significant ashfall and large tephra (volcanic glass/reticulite) fallout across East Hawaii, including Volcano Village and Hilo. The eruption, featuring 1,500-foot lava fountains, caused flight cancellations and road closures along Highway 11.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Episode 41 of lava fountaining from the north and south vents in the Halemaumau crater on the summit of Kilauea began at 11:10 a.m. HST on January 24 and lasted eight hours. Tephra fallout of various sizes were reported in public viewing areas within Hawaii Volcanoes National Park, Volcano Village, Mauna Loa Estates and Ohia Estates. Tephra sizes ranged from ash of less than 1/16 inch in diameter to bombs larger than 2.5 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front moved down the island chain from January 20th through January 22nd. The front stalled in the vicinity of Maui County on January 21st, with the highest rain of 1 to 3 inches impacting many statewide north and east facing slopes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two hikers were rescued by helicopter near mile marker 5 of the Hana Highway due to rising stream levels cutting them off from the rest of their group.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below average rainfall allowed severe to extreme drought conditions to continue across portions of the Big Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D3 extreme drought existed over portions of the Hamakua District. Below average monthly rainfall led to continued adverse agricultural impacts across northern pastures.  Supplemental livestock feeding was required due to persistent water shortages, especially in the Hamakua and Kau Districts. Ranchers weaned cattle early and destocked cows due to lack of available pasture feed, as well as having to haul water in and move cattle to the Kona District for better grazing conditions. Many reports cite not seeing conditions like this since around 2008.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low brought heavy rain to portions of the Big Island, which led to localized flooding over the Kawa Flats.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mamalohoa Hwy was closed between mile markers 57 and 61 due to water on the road.
